Reynolds number based on louver pitch.

can be correlated within +/- 15%.

coilDesigner.value:
Reference:  Park, Hrnjak; CO2 and R410A flow boiling heat transfer, pressure drop, and flow pattern at low  
temperatures in a horizontal smooth tube. International Journal of Refrigeration 30 (2007) 166-178

Flow boiling heat transfer coefficient, pressure drop, and flow pattern are investigated in the horizontal smooth  
tube of 6.1 mm inner diameter for CO2, R410A, and R22. Flow boiling heat transfer coefficients are measured at the  
constant wall temperature conditions, while pressure drop measurement and flow visualization are carried out at  
adiabatic conditions. This research is performed at evaporation temperatures of _15 and _30 _C, mass flux from 100  
to 400 kg m_2 s_1, and heat flux
from 5 to 15 kWm_2 for vapor qualities ranging from 0.1 to 0.8

coilDesigner.value:
Reference:	"An Investigation of Refrigerant Void Fraction in Horizontal, Microfin Tubes,"
D.A. Yashar, M.J. Wilson, H.R. Kopke, D.M. Graham, J.C. Chato, T.A. Newell.International Journal of HVAC & R Research 107 (Part 2), 173-188. Preprinted copy found in ASHRAE Transactions, June 2001, Paper #4472.

Mass-flux-dependent model that uses a "Froude rate" parameter defined by Graham et al. (1998)
in combination with the Lockhart-Martinelli parameter.  It can be used for the stratified to
annular flow region.  It was found to model the void fraction in microfin tubes well, especially
evaporators.

coilDesigner.name: TFVoidFractionCorrelations.Hughmark
coilDesigner.value:
The Hughmark Void Fraction model equations are solved iteratively to find the void fraction.
It is therefore the most complicated and time-consuming model.
It is a generalization of Bankoff's correlation (1960).  Hughmark found it to work well for
horizontal flow, even though it was developed for vertical upward flow in air-liquid systems.

References:	"The Effect of Void Fraction Correlation and Heat Flux Assumption on Refrigerant
Charge Inventory Predictions," C.K. Rice.  ASHRAE Transactions, Vol. 93, Part 1,
1987, pp. 341-367.
"Holdup in gas-liquid flow," G.A. Hughmark.  Chemical Engineering Progress, mVol. 58, No. 4, April 1962, pp. 62-65.

coilDesigner.name: TFRTPDPCorrelations.XuFang2013RTPEvapDP
coilDesigner.value:
Xu-Fang Two-Phase Frictional Pressure Drop Correlation for Evaporating Flow in Pipes

Yu Xu, Xiande Fang, 2013, "A new correlation of two-phase frictional pressure drop for evaporating flow in pipes",
Nuclear Engineering and Design 263 (2013) 87– 96

A comprehensive survey of correlations and experimental investigations of two-phase frictional pressure drop is
conducted. The 2622 experimental data points of 15 refrigerants are obtained, with hydraulic diameter from 0.81 to 19.1 mm, mass flux from 25.4 to 1150 kg m 2 s 1, and heat flux from 0.6 to 150 kW m 2. There are 29 existing correlations evaluated against the experimental database. It is found that the best correlation has a mean absolute relative deviation (MARD) of 28.5%. Based on the experimental database, a new correlation is proposed, which has an MARD of 25.2%, improving remarkably the prediction of two-phase frictional pressure drop for pipe evaporating flow, especially for micro-channels.

Includes 15 refrigerants, ammonia, R11, R12, R22, R32, R125, R32/R125 (0.6/0.4,wt), R123, R134a, R407C, R410A, R507, R600a, R290 and CO2.

coilDesigner.name: MCRTPHTCCorrelations.PetersKandlikar2007MCEvapRTPHTC
coilDesigner.value:
Peters proposed modifications to the use of Kandlikar's Heat Transfer correlation for Evaporaion with deep laminar flows.

Julian V. S. Peters & Satish G. Kandlikar, 2007, "FURTHER EVALUATION OF A FLOW BOILING CORRELATION FOR MICROCHANNELS AND MINICHANNELS", Proceedings of the Fifth International Conference on Nanochannels, Microchannels and Minichannels, CNMM2007-30027, June 18-20, 2007, Puebla, Mexico.

For liquid-only Reynolds numbers between 100 and 410, only the NBD correlation applies. For very low Reynolds numbers, generally under 100, the convective boiling term is removed from the NBD correlation.


Also refer to 
SATISH G. KANDLIKAR & PRABHU BALASUBRAMANIAN, 2004, "An Extension of the Flow Boiling Correlation to Transition, Laminar, and Deep Laminar Flows in Minichannels and Microchannels", Heat Transfer Engineering, 25(3):86–93, 2004

coilDesigner.name: TFRTPTHCCorrelations.GrecoVanoliTFRTPHTC
coilDesigner.value:
Greco and Vanoli proposed modifications to the use of Kandlikar's Heat Transfer correlation for Evaporaion with deep laminar flows.

Greco a, G.P. Vanoli b, 2005, "Flow boiling heat transfer with HFC mixtures in a smooth horizontal tube. Part II: Assessment of predictive methods", Experimental Thermal and Fluid Science 29 (2005) 199–208

R410A and R404A have been experimentally investigated in a smooth horizontal tube made of stainless steel with an inner diameter of 6 mm and a length of 6 m, uniformly heated by the Joule effect. The evaporation pressures has been varied within the range from 3 to 12 bar, the refrigerant mass flux within the range from 290 to 1100 kg/m2 s and the heat flux within the range from 11 to 39 kW/m2. A modification of the Kandlikar correlation has been proposed to predict the local heat transfer coefficients obtained with the test facility. A correction factor that enhance the influence of the nucleate boiling term has been introduced to take into account the influence of the reduced pressure on the heat transfer coefficients.

Also refer to 
SATISH G. KANDLIKAR & PRABHU BALASUBRAMANIAN, 2004, "An Extension of the Flow Boiling Correlation to Transition, Laminar, and Deep Laminar Flows in Minichannels and Microchannels", Heat Transfer Engineering, 25(3):86–93, 2004

coilDesigner.name: HXTTRLHTCCorrelations.ArnoldEtAl_FlutedAnnulusRLHTC
coilDesigner.value:
Arnold et al (1993), Single phase HTC

Reference: Arnold, J.A., Garimella, S., Christenen, R.N., 1993. A Manual for Heat Exchanger Design Using Spirally Fluted Tubes

Valid for:
0.104 < e_star < 0.384
0.383 < pstar < 6.714
0.279 < theta_star < 0.652
0.216 < rstar < 0.604
800 < Re < 40,000
Valid for annulus side

e_star = (Flute Depth) / (Inner Flute Vol OD);
pstar = (Flute Pitch) / (Inner Flute Vol OD);
theta = arctan(pi * (Inner Flute Vol OD) / (No. of Flute Starts * Flute Pitch));
theta_star = theta / (pi / 2); (since theta is in radians)

rstar = Inner Flute Vol OD / Inner flute volume based ID;

Inner Flute Vol OD = Inner flute volume based ID + 2*Inner Tube Wall Thickness

This correlation is a unified formula for all pipe flow regimes from laminar to fully turbulent
Application Range:
Dh = 6mm tested 
Mass flux = 20-(14*10^6) kg/m^2*s 
Flow Regimes categories: Smooth Laminar, Transition, Smooth  turbulent, Non fully developed Partially Turbulent, Rough Turbulent 

Not applicable for smooth tubes. Must specify a non-zero roughness.

coilDesigner.name: MCRLHTCCorrelations.Gnielinski2013PortBasedLamNuMCRLHTC
coilDesigner.value:
It uses the original Gnielinski MC-RLHTC correlation and adds smoothing as per the 2013 Gnielinski paper

Gnielinski, 2013, On heat transfer in Tubes, Intl J of Heat and Mass Transfer, Vol 63, pp. 134-140.
Compared to previous correlation, this one adds smoothing between constant Nu and the fully turbulent region in particular between Re = 2300 and 4000.
